paragraphs.body: In the report, “1972: Joanne Pierce Misko and Susan Roley Malone”, Tricia Wellman proudly presents two powerful women named Joanne Pierce Misko and Susan Roley Malone and their historic achievement. Wellman also talks about the obstacles and challenges they had to go through to becoming one of the first female FBI agents to ever exist in a field dominated by men. She displays her point by developing several important ideas. First, Wellman explains how both individuals had to deal with doubts and criticism from men around them, but later both Misko and Malone showed their strength by overcoming these challenges. Next, she discusses their dedication and willpower in proving men wrong by meeting the physical and training standards on the men’s scale defining all odds and proving how men and women can be equal. Finally, Wellman emphasizes on the real commitment of Misko and Malone in being identified as special agents, not to just seek special treatment just because they were women, but to follow the dreams they’ve longed devoted to.

annotations.text: Finally, Wellman emphasizes on the real commitment of Misko and Malone in being identified as special agents, not to just seek special treatment just because they were women, but to follow the dreams they’ve longed devoted to.

paragraphs.body: After reading about Misko and Malone, I’ve really enabled myself to believe that they’re what true perfection is. They fought though all hardships, became big figures of their success, and nothing seems to be bringing them down. Describing them as perfection would be the condition of being free from flaws or defects; the highest standard of quality or excellence in completing a specific task. Almost like a robot per say, even the definition sounds robotic but is that what I truly strive to be?

annotations.comment: I'm not quite seeing the connection between Misko and Malone's achievement and the idea of robotic perfection? I wonder if this paragraph is necessary for your essay's purpose...
annotations.context_left: what true perfection is. They fought though all hardships, became

paragraphs.body: Being raised in a Chinese household I was always driven by success and perfection to the point that my parents think that I can do whatever they ask me to. Translations? Got it. Good Grades? Here you go. Intensive studying? Do I have a choice. There was always competition in anything I partake in because of the environment I was raised in, everything resorted to me doing better than anyone else because that was everything my mother wanted. It was truly overwhelming, but they wouldn’t have understood my feelings. Ever since my childhood my parents and grandparents had high expectations for me. They were always seeking perfection out of me, knowing that I was the girl in the family, that I was the one who would bring them success. Especially my grandparents, who were so influenced by Chinese culture, since they were from Fuzhou, China. They carried a lot of that culture into how they raised me.

annotations.text: Translations? Got it. Good Grades? Here you go. Intensive studying? Do I have a choice.
Especially my grandparents, who were so influenced by Chinese culture, since they were from Fuzhou, China.
annotations.comment: this is a sentence frag, but it's also interesting. Why is China culture, or Fuzhou in particular, so focused on perfection? Perhaps your reader could use some context?

paragraphs.body: Perfection in my childhood wasn’t just about getting good grades, it was also influenced by my family’s cultural beliefs. My grandmother thinks that good grades would also lead to me finding a wealthy husband. She believes that having a perfect daughter is important so that I can attract a successful man. No one in the family thinks women should work, they believe women should only carry a child and be a stay-at-home wife (I despise that idea). Arranged marriages are very common in Chinese culture. Both my grandparents and my parents had arranged marriages, so it would only make sense for them to want the same for me. And that is true, my grandma already has an arranged marriage set up for me, I feel deep sorrow just thinking about it. Will I not be able to pursue my dream of being an FBI agent and follow along Malone’s path? Is this truly the perfection that I’ve seeked for my whole life through all the manipulation? I’ve gone through so many hardships since I was a kid, and it would only make sense if I explained it.

annotations.text: grandma already has an arranged marriage set up for me, I feel deep sorrow just thinking about it. Will I not be able to pursue my dream of being an FBI agent and follow along Malone’s path?
annotations.comment: fascinating (and see my comment, above, about cultural expectations)

This paragraph is a good move to provide context, but perhaps it's in the wrong logical order? Also, I wonder if this essay is less about "perfection," exactly, and more about defying the expectations for women your culture and family expects of you?

paragraphs.body: My parents made me follow an intensive study system starting as early as sixth grade, right after I finished elementary school. I was forced to go to prep school for three hours every single day after regular school ended, and that went on until I became a senior. To be honest, I never knew what the word "freedom" even meant because I was so controlled by my parents. I had no time for myself. I would get home as late as 8 p.m., just to sleep and do it all over again the next day.

annotations.text: To be honest, I never knew what the word "freedom" even meant because I was so controlled by my parents.
annotations.comment: Yes. Again: fascinating. I'm interested in how becoming an FBI agent is connected to freedom for you--to living your own life and making your own choices (this is why I don't think this essay is really about "perfection).
annotations.context_right: I had no time for m

paragraphs.body: On top of that, they always compared me to my brother. Honestly, when it comes to perfection, I thought he was perfect. He never went to prep school like I did, but he scored higher on the SAT than me. He did things so effortlessly and robotically, he never studied, just played video games all day, and still got 100s on every test. Watching him get the praise I always wanted made me so mad. Why him? Why not me?

But now, I realized what true perfection is to me. It isn’t about comparing myself to my brother and admitting he’s better. Susan Malone didn’t lowly compare herself with other male cadets. She never found ways to bring herself down, instead she always thought of the positives. That’s what true perfection is. For me, instead of constantly following on the footsteps of my parents, I strive to do what I believe will make me perfect. According to Wellman she stated “Her manager at the time explained all the pros and cons of the job and supported her application. Susan Roley Malone had already broken stereotypes as a U.S. Marine” Wellman’s main point in this phrase was to tell me that despite all odds of what her manager said, she broke the barrier and exceeded all the expectations. If I want to succeed perfection and be the version of myself, I need to break all odds. My dream is to be an FBI agent, not the first but I want to be the first to break a connection between my parents and me and not play by the rules. Everything about my life is about crime scene, criminals, and investigations. I’ve done so much research I’ve committed my whole body to that field since I was young and I will use all the knowledge I gained from my childhood to exceed my expectations. Not my mom’s expectations but mine. Another point Wellman said was “FBI Director J. Edgar Hoover had a firm rule: only men could be special agents in the FBI.” This proves that Malone also played against the rules. Just as I stated before I will play against my mother’s firm rule and strive to be the FBI Agent I’ve always dreamed to be.

annotations.text: Just as I stated before I will play against my mother’s firm rule and strive to be the FBI Agent I’ve always dreamed to
annotations.comment: You see how the essay is recognizing that there are "rules" for men and women and you don't want to be limited by them?

paragraphs.body: Perfection isn’t supposed to be a robotic behavior. I’ve had that idea for the longest times, perfection isn’t just about being flawless or having no defects. Being perfect for yourself is when you realize everything starts coming together like a puzzle piece, its something that is fulfilled with a purpose. It’s more than just skills or achievements, it’s about the journey of personal growth. Striving for perfection is about appreciating every step along the way, not just reaching a perfect end goal.

In terms of images: your images all seem to be slightly comical or juvenile. Your essay is clearly about the strength and power of women--real women in the real world--(including yourself) and of your desire in breaking free of gendered limitations. I wonder if more realistic images, more mature photos, more powerful photos would better develop your ideas? Citation https://www.intelligence.gov/people/barrier-breakers-in-history/659-1972-joanne-pierce-misko-and-susan-roley-malone
